author: @qabsoloff3252
text: As you say, the large amount of information is the boon and bane of the internet. The real problem is the inability to effectively search that body of information. Search engines today actively prevent accurate searches. The search engine providers want to lead users to money making sites. One used to be able to use specific search modifiers that allowed a search to be specific. No longer. With respect to AC to DC or vice versa, my favourite method was that used in older elevator systems. They used an AC motor to drive a DC motor/gen. Very simple and reliable. Of course using a DC motor to drive a AC motor/gen did the opposite. AC<>DC with no electronics.

author: @lloydevans2900
text:
As I understand it, a pair of metal plates (both of the same metal, aluminium in the example you showed) immersed in an electrolyte is not really an electrolytic capacitor, or at least would be a really inefficient one with absurdly poor performance. The reason being that the electrolyte is conductive, so it would not be able to store a charge.

If you applied a DC voltage between the plates, you would make an electrolytic cell - the applied voltage would electrolyse the solution, so any charge would be dissipated. It might be able to store a small amount of charge at low voltage, up to a maximum of whatever voltage would be required for the easiest electrolytic reaction to occur. With sodium bicarbonate, this would probably be the voltage required to electrolyse water, which is only about 1.5 volts - so you would only be able to store charge for any length of time at voltages lower than that.

If you applied AC between the plates, you would make an "electrode boiler" - the voltage would still be dissipated, but the result would be heating up the electrolyte rather than driving any electrochemical reactions. Commercially available electrode boilers exist (you can find them on Ebay for fairly cheap), and are essentially the same - a pair of metal plates (usually stainless steel) held a few centimetres apart within a plastic frame, with the plates directly connected to the live and neutral wires of a mains electrical cable. These don't work too well with pure water, but only require the addition of a tiny bit of soluble salt (sodium chloride, sodium carbonate, magnesium sulfate) to make the water conductive - a few hundred milligrams in a litre of water is enough to get a decent current flow, with either 120 or 240 volts mains AC.

You can use a pair of aluminium electrodes and a saturated sodium bicarbonate solution as an electrolytic capacitor, with a subtle but important modification: Only one electrode plate can be immersed in the electrolyte, the other must be stuck to the exterior surface of the electrolyte container, such as a glass jar or plastic bottle. This means there is no conductive pathway between the electrodes, which is what allows a charge to be stored.

Thanks for this cool video! The ability to make a half wave rectifier from something as simple as a strip of aluminum, strip of lead and some sodium bicarbonate solution is very impressive. 
But, yes, there is a huge amount of information on the internet... and just like in books, not everything you read is true.

author: @michaelsohocki1573
text:
Dear Rob, I come from an age of had-to-do.
(And so we DID. All over the place--every which way it fit and a few ways it didn't.) My motivation to dig came out of a perception of need.

Skip forward 35 years, my children come from an age of don't-have-to-do. 
(And so, they don't.) It was never my pRents yelling and screaming to get off the couch that made me do so. I WANTED to get off the couch and get up to my elbows in something.

How do we create that perception of need in our children, in a world where nothing is needed?

author: @davidtansley1635
text:
Hi Robert,
Your ascertion that the internet is just the start of the journey is correct and profound.  
I had the pleasure, challenge, and issues of being a postgraduate subject head for four years.  My first lecture opened with the welcome, followed with:
'This is postgraduate studies.  Therefore, I expect you to be proficient in defining problems, research, using the research to solve the problem.  I expect you to identify issues that are at the heart of the problem, to look deeper, and utilising all of your previous studies to provide real solutions  from your experience and further a fully rounded solution.
Google is not a reference.  Any perpeople who sites Google as a reference will receive an automatic fail.
Lastly, your subject outline states that, at my discretion, you may be permitted a formula sheet in the final examination.  As there are only 15 formulas, I expect them to be committed to memory, and therefore, i exclude the formula sheet.
Previously, when formula sheets  were permitted, students would spend countless hours writting the solutions to the textbook problems only to enter the examination to find none of the text questions on the examination paper because I write them and solve them.  You are best served by preparing yourself of the lecture content prior to my lectures.  If the lecture does not answer your questions, you can ask intelligent questions and site your investigations to solve your question and based upon the information you provide, I can either point you into the correct direction, or correct you you thinking process to enable you to gather the research to satisfy you curiosity and question.

author: @Alkimi
text: My favorite thing to do when I go into town is take an armful of books to the book exchange in LA and leave hours later with an armful of different books. I've found the most incredible old books there. And sometimes they have little bits of history stuck between the pages! One of my prize possessions is a book called 'Radio Chemistry'. The foreword was written by Marie Curie (not to be confused with Mercury 😅) and the book is all about radioactive substances and decay particles, but nothing is mentioned about the dangers of any of it, because it was published in the 1920s!

replies.author: @Alkimi
replies.text: Oh yeah and I found an electricians union membership card from the 30s stuck in the pages.

author: @stephenroot1012
text: There were also mechanical rectifiers that used ac motors. One was a clock like motor that turned a rotating disk switches in sync with the AC current and only closed the contacts when the current was in one direction to the load and opened it when the current reversed ( used for charging lead acid batteries if I remember right ). The other one used an AC motor to turn a DC generator which was then used by the load.
